replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: this kinda touches into what i was saying about running a xylene/styrene mixture through an inkjet printer head, though to make it more friendly, the print chamber should be sealed and a cycling of air from inside there should be pulled through a condenser to recapture the xylene as it evaporates and pump the air devoid of the xylene back into the chamber so accept more of the solvent then continue carrying it away, heck with that system you could possibly just keep feeding styrene feed stock into a sort of squid system that feeds into an inkjet printer head and just keep printing using the same solvent for quite a long time :) and you use a thin enough mix to run it through very high resolution inkjet heads that are 1200 to 2400 dpi sized (not to be confused with placement of things like printers that have numbers ranging up to 9600,though that can be used to make smoother laydown) and you end up with some incredible results that finally reaches the level of finished production with out needing a bunch of cleaning or extra work on the part, just pull it out and use it :)

author: @roidroid
text:
Shrinkage might be a bit of a show stopper.  3d printers print onto a heated bed to minimize shrinkage before the whole piece is finished, uneven shrinkage tends to warp and bow the print - the result it that it's doesn't print flat and can ruin the print entirely if it bows up and crashes into the print-head.  A heated bed may make your liquid plastic dry even quicker though, making the problem worse.

Some possible solutions:
- Use as little of the solvent as you can, to get the viscosity really high so it will stack up without needing to dry, and _(as you already said)_ to reduce shrinkage. 
- Print into an air-tight print chamber filled with vaporized solvent (to slow down the drying), an oven bag might work for that assuming it itself doesn't dissolve.  It's common to use acetone vapor to smooth the surface of finished ABS prints, and oven bags have been used with success to create heated build chambers.
- I've never heard of a COOLED print bed, but it's certainly doable and might be of use here.   _(Some people already use thermocouples to heat the bed, they could just run them backwards and put a heatsink on the back)_.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yes I agree 100% and I have been thinking about this - as you know I am coming at this from a chemistry, paints, inks background and shrinkage is a serious problem there too - My answer is likely to be based on the use of inert filler - two reasons really - it will reduce shrinkage and reduce cost and filler material can be modified easily to produce plastics of varying functional characteristics. But, in my estimation it is going to be the main problem to solve - that and the actual delivery system - but then isn't that what experimentation is about? Anyway very good input mate and thanks for that. It's good to hear from someone who 'gets' the problem - it might seem like a bit of a silly thing to say but so many people don't it really is nice to get someone who does and get a bit of valuable feedback - Rob.

replies.author: @roidroid
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
the most common types of paste extruding 3d printers use a simple plunger/syringe delivery system, they commonly print chocolate, cake icing, silicone, or the ceramic/metal pastes you already mentioned.  I'd expect your liquid plastic to work in them without modification, assuming the syringe doesn't dissolve over the long term.

I'm more familiar with plastic printing than paste printing (and even with plastic printers i'm not too great).  But something i'm curious about is if paste printers could be faster than plastic printers, i'll have to look into this.  Almost all of the aspects which limit the speed of plastic filament printers are related to the need to achieve steady and even heating/cooling, either in the extruder hot-end or on the print-bed.  Since paste extruders avoid all heating, they may be able to print incredibly fast.  If true, then another way of avoiding your liquid plastic prints warping could be to just print incredibly fast, aiming to finish the entire print before any significant shrinking or solidification starts.

I know nothing of the chemistry, can i ask a question: If you could use a lower volatility solvent which dries slower, and also aim for a very thick paste which (while un-dried) can stack up very high without drooping (perhaps this is what you're talking about with your fillers - a suspension?), would this be possible and/or helpful?

hmm... wonder if there's any good *sheer-thinning* liquids which could dry (or be baked, like with metal pastes) into a fused solid... ~~ponder ponder ponder~~.  If i start to make a ketchup 3d printer, could someone please stop me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: roidroid They are all good points mate - i am going to have to look into the performance characteristics of paste printers of which i knew nothing until you mentioned them. I did a video on microwave fusion of plastics with small amounts of susceptors added - now i am thinking a high viscosity paste with 1% susceptor material and a microwave cure might be possible - we are talking home microwave here - as a possible route. Sheer addition might be something like xanthan gum which takes 0.5% addition only. Unfortunately most plastic solvents are going to be volatile liquids or stupidly expensive - and we are talking hundreds of pound per liter - this won't get adopted unless it is at least the same price as current methodologies - or better yet - cheaper. The filler idea will certainly reduce the cost and is standard practice in the plastics industry i believe - still - a lot to think about in your post mate - thanks

replies.author: @roidroid
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Should be a ton of stuff here to show you what's been done and how successful it was.  Lots of categories, links and sub-links to drill through for the motivated, good luck:  http://www.reprap.org/wiki/Category:Paste

author: @m3sca1
text: I suppose this method could be done with epoxy if some sort of disposable mixing nozzle was employed...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suppose it could - have you tried it?

replies.author: @m3sca1
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith LOL no I have not- if I had, I would not be making suppositions.
I was just throwing ideas around.
Please don't take me the wrong way.
I like your solvent dissolved plastic printing idea. It's just that epoxy jumped into my head when i watched your video, after you mentioned the shrinkage problem.
Also the idea of adding air to make foam into the nozzle popped into my head.
That might reduce the shrinkage and make the raw material go further, by making a sponge like material instead of a solid.
Just ideas and brainstorming mate.
I owe you a debt of gratitude, on the back of your graphene videos and so when i watch your vids i like to share my ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: m3sca1 Equally don't get me wrong the epoxy idea is a good idea. Just not relevant here. When trying to move something from concept to actual you have to concentrate on simplicity - over thinking the problem just muddies the water and over engineering the answer is just as bad. Extensions like foaming are something to think about once the base system is in place. Focus my friend - that's what makes the difference between an inventor and a dreamer.

replies.author: @m3sca1
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith The whole concept of squirting goo out of a nozzle to print a thing is the concept here, so how is it not relevant to suggest squirting epoxy goo out of a nozzle to print a thing?
A shrinkage problem,from an engineers perspective is very big issue when considering the final size of a printed product.
If the software has to factor in a shrinkage error, that's not a very slick solution.
Deploying your idea is as simple as getting a filament printer and replacing the filament feed motor with a peristaltic pump and mucking around with nozzle size.
It is the same as concrete printing.
It has inherent speed issues due to curing or drying times.
Whereas thermomelt is faster.
So epoxy set printing is the perfect thing to use on a slow speed wet deposition printer because once the epoxy reaction has started,it will have rigidity.
Evaporation of solvent set printing is subject to flaws like slumping from evaporating solvent pooling up and softening parts of the build.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: m3sca1 I am not sure now how to reply to you. You are clearly feeling offended and I am sorry for that - but that isn't the whole concept - the whole concept is making solvent casting a viable print method
